text: The surgeon aligns the divided transverse colon and terminal ileum in an isoparastaltic configuration, positioning the specimen above the right foot and below the liver for the ileocolic resection.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to measure and align the terminal ileal staple line, counting back two centimeters to create the aneurotomy. The robotic system provides significant magnification, enhancing the precision of the surgical actions.

The surgeon uses cautery to cut the terminal ileum two centimeters back at the anti-mesenteric border, minimizing the risk of involving the mesentery. On the transverse colon side, the fenestrated bipolar is used to measure six centimeters back, where a colotomy is made slightly off the tinea libra due to the presence of epiploic fat.

The surgeon inserts the left hand with one jaw inside the colotomy and one jaw outside, using this position for efficient exposure. The cartridge end of the stapler is slid into the colon, aligning it perfectly. The terminal ileum is brought to the stapler, with the butt of the stapler dropped down and the tip raised up. The surgeon uses the blunt edge of a grasper to milk the bowel onto the crotch of the stapler.

The surgeon clamps down on the mesentery to ensure proper alignment and absence of twisting. After firing the stapler, the surgeon begins to sew the common enterotomy closed, using two Alice clamps to secure the corners of the staple line.

The surgeon performs a full thickness U-stitch on either side of the staple line, taking small but complete bites through the colonic tissue. The suture is placed from the outside to the inside on the colonic side, and then from inside to outside on the top side, emphasizing the importance of the bottom side suture.

The surgeon emphasizes the importance of exposing the back wall thoroughly to prevent leaks. A full thickness suture is tied down using a 2-0 Vicryl for traction. The common enterotomy is closed using a 3-0 V-lock suture on a V-20 needle, employing a technique from open surgery to sew in two layers, first from the top down and then from the bottom up.

The surgeon begins suturing at the top of the incision using a V-lock suture. Initially, the barbed tines are not engaged, requiring cinching after the second and third throws. The assistant pulls up on the upper suture to open the incision, creating a 'fish mouth' appearance for better visibility of the sewing area.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar device to measure distances for enterotomies, demonstrating the two-centimeter and six-centimeter points. The suture exits the small bowel at a distance equivalent to the width of the fenestrated bipolar, approximately five millimeters. The needle exits the small bowel at a similar distance from the mucosal edge, emphasizing the importance of taking five-millimeter bites to prevent tissue ischemia and potential leaks.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in arm number three to apply traction on the inferior suture, then transfers it to the left hand. The needle is positioned to pierce the colonic side, and the left hand rotates to complete the closure of the enterotomy. The surgeon prepares to run a second layer of closure using a running technique.
The surgeon uses their left hand to grab the V-lock suture and rotates it ninety degrees. With an open needle driver, they cinch down the suture until the barbed tines are no longer visible.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar device to atraumatically grasp and manipulate the tissue while suturing the anastomosis. The suture is carefully pinched, rotated, and cinched to secure the closure, avoiding fracture by not pinching with the needle driver. The suture is cut flush with the serosa, and ICG is injected to check for perfusion, confirming the anastomosis is well-perfused and undamaged.
